texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Multicomponent gas  separation by selective surface flow  (SSF) and poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membranes
texts.text: Multicomponent gas  separation by selective surface flow  (SSF) and poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: M. Anand, M. Langsam, M.B. Rao, S.  Sircar  *
texts.text: M. Anand, M. Langsam, M.B. Rao, S.  Sircar  *

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  selective surface flow  (SSF)  membrane consisting of  a  thin  layer of  a  nanoporous  carbon  was produced  in  a tubular form using  a macroporous  alumina  support. The membrane  was tested for  hydrogen  enrichment  applications. Simulated waste  gases  from a  petrochemical refinery and  a  hydrogen  pressure swing  adsorption unit were  used  as  the feed  gas  to  the membrane. Very  high  rejections of  C  1-C3 hydrocarbons (saturated and  unsaturated) and  carbon  dioxide over  hydrogen  were exhibited by  the  membrane at  low  feed  gas  pressures. The hydrogen  enriched stream was produced  at  the  feed  gas  pressure.
texts.text: A  selective surface flow  (SSF)  membrane consisting of  a  thin  layer of  a  nanoporous  carbon  was produced  in  a tubular form using  a macroporous  alumina  support. The membrane  was tested for  hydrogen  enrichment  applications. Simulated waste  gases  from a  petrochemical refinery and  a  hydrogen  pressure swing  adsorption unit were  used  as  the feed  gas  to  the membrane. Very  high  rejections of  C  1-C3 hydrocarbons (saturated and  unsaturated) and  carbon  dioxide over  hydrogen  were exhibited by  the  membrane at  low  feed  gas  pressures. The hydrogen  enriched stream was produced  at  the  feed  gas  pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation characteristics of  a  polymeric poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membrane in  a tubular form was also tested for  the  same applications using  identical conditions of  operation. This  membrane  also  selectively rejected heavier components  of  the feed  gas  mixture over  hydrogen  and  produced the  hydrogen  enriched stream at  the feed  gas  pressure. The SSF membrane exhibited much higher hydrogen  recovery and  hydrocarbon rejections than  the  PTMSP membrane for  these applications under  identical conditions of  operations using  identical support materials.
texts.text: The separation characteristics of  a  polymeric poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membrane in  a tubular form was also tested for  the  same applications using  identical conditions of  operation. This  membrane  also  selectively rejected heavier components  of  the feed  gas  mixture over  hydrogen  and  produced the  hydrogen  enriched stream at  the feed  gas  pressure. The SSF membrane exhibited much higher hydrogen  recovery and  hydrocarbon rejections than  the  PTMSP membrane for  these applications under  identical conditions of  operations using  identical support materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Gas separations; Selective surface flow; PTMSP  membranes
texts.text: Keywords: Gas separations; Selective surface flow; PTMSP  membranes

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 1.  Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  nanoporous  carbon  membrane  called selective surface  flow  (SSF)  membrane for separation  of gaseous  mixtures  is  developed  by Air  Products  and Chemicals [1-7].  It  consists of a thin  (1-3 /xm) layer of  a nanoporous  carbon  (5-7  A  pore  diameter) material produced by carbonization of poly-vinylidene chloride latex  film  on a macroporous support [5-7]. The membrane  selectively adsorbs  the  larger or more polar  components of a gas mixture  on the pore walls  at  the  high pressure side. The  adsorbed molecules selectively diffuse  on  the pore surface
texts.text: A  nanoporous  carbon  membrane  called selective surface  flow  (SSF)  membrane for separation  of gaseous  mixtures  is  developed  by Air  Products  and Chemicals [1-7].  It  consists of a thin  (1-3 /xm) layer of  a nanoporous  carbon  (5-7  A  pore  diameter) material produced by carbonization of poly-vinylidene chloride latex  film  on a macroporous support [5-7]. The membrane  selectively adsorbs  the  larger or more polar  components of a gas mixture  on the pore walls  at  the  high pressure side. The  adsorbed molecules selectively diffuse  on  the pore surface

texts.label: footnote
texts.text: *  Corresponding author.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: across  the  membrane  to the low pressure side  and then desorb.  Thus, the membrane  produces a  gas stream  enriched in  the  smaller or  less polar molecules at  the  high  pressure side  and  a gas  stream  enriched in the  larger or more polar  molecules  at  the  low pressure  side. The membrane  is  found to  be very  effective  for  enriching hydrogen from feed gas streams containing 20-50 mole%  H 2 at  a low to moderate (30-200  psig)  total gas pressure. The other  components  of  the  feed  gas  stream  can  be:  (a)  a mixture  of C1-C  4 hydrocarbons  as in  the  case  of a fluid catalytic cracker (FCC) off-gas [3]; or  (b)  a mixture  of CO 2 and CH  4  as  in the case of a pressure  swing adsorption (PSA) off-gas for  hydrogen purification [41.
texts.text: across  the  membrane  to the low pressure side  and then desorb.  Thus, the membrane  produces a  gas stream  enriched in  the  smaller or  less polar molecules at  the  high  pressure side  and  a gas  stream  enriched in the  larger or more polar  molecules  at  the  low pressure  side. The membrane  is  found to  be very  effective  for  enriching hydrogen from feed gas streams containing 20-50 mole%  H 2 at  a low to moderate (30-200  psig)  total gas pressure. The other  components  of  the  feed  gas  stream  can  be:  (a)  a mixture  of C1-C  4 hydrocarbons  as in  the  case  of a fluid catalytic cracker (FCC) off-gas [3]; or  (b)  a mixture  of CO 2 and CH  4  as  in the case of a pressure  swing adsorption (PSA) off-gas for  hydrogen purification [41.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  high free volume, glassy  poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  polymeric membrane also  selectively permeates larger molecules of  a  gas  mixture with  moderate  to  high  permeance  and  permselectivities  over the smaller  molecules  [8-10]. Thus, its separation characteristics are  similar to  those  of  the SSF membranes. The material has  been extensively studied and  a  porous  open  structure is  conceptualized to  explain its unusual  permeation properties [11,12]. The membrane has  been  evaluated for  separation of larger hydrocarbons (C  3  and  C  4)  from  methane  as  a method to  fractionate natural gas  [11].
texts.text: The  high free volume, glassy  poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  polymeric membrane also  selectively permeates larger molecules of  a  gas  mixture with  moderate  to  high  permeance  and  permselectivities  over the smaller  molecules  [8-10]. Thus, its separation characteristics are  similar to  those  of  the SSF membranes. The material has  been extensively studied and  a  porous  open  structure is  conceptualized to  explain its unusual  permeation properties [11,12]. The membrane has  been  evaluated for  separation of larger hydrocarbons (C  3  and  C  4)  from  methane  as  a method to  fractionate natural gas  [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  purpose of this  paper is to compare the separation performances of  SSF and PTMSP membranes  for  hydrogen  enrichment  applications using FCC  and  PSA  off-gases as  feed  streams.
texts.text: The  purpose of this  paper is to compare the separation performances of  SSF and PTMSP membranes  for  hydrogen  enrichment  applications using FCC  and  PSA  off-gases as  feed  streams.

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 2.  Experimental  methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both the  SSF  and the  PTMSP membranes were produced in tubular forms for  the tests. Identical macroporous  alumina  tubes  (30 cm  long,  0.56  cm internal diameter, 0.165 cm  thick  wall, < 1 /zm pores  on tube  walls) were  used  as  supports (Support A)  for  the membranes. The  SSF  membrane  was produced  by:  (a)  coating the  bore  side of  the  tubular support  with  a thin  uniform  layer of  a poly(vinylidene chloride)-acrylate terpolymer latex which contained  0.1-0.14 /xm polymer  beads in an aqueous emulsion  (4  wt%  solid); (b)  drying the  coat under  N 2
texts.text: Both the  SSF  and the  PTMSP membranes were produced in tubular forms for  the tests. Identical macroporous  alumina  tubes  (30 cm  long,  0.56  cm internal diameter, 0.165 cm  thick  wall, < 1 /zm pores  on tube  walls) were  used  as  supports (Support A)  for  the membranes. The  SSF  membrane  was produced  by:  (a)  coating the  bore  side of  the  tubular support  with  a thin  uniform  layer of  a poly(vinylidene chloride)-acrylate terpolymer latex which contained  0.1-0.14 /xm polymer  beads in an aqueous emulsion  (4  wt%  solid); (b)  drying the  coat under  N 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: at  50°C:  and  (c)  heating under  a  N 2  purge  to  600°C for  carbonizing the  polymer.  A  detailed description of the carbonization process  is given elsewhere [1,2,5-7]. Only  a  single coat of  the  latex and  a  single heating operation were  used.  The PTMSP membrane was produced  by:  (a)  coating the  bore  side  of  the alumina support  with a 0.5 wt% solution of the polymer in toluene; (b) air  drying;  and (c)  final drying  under  dry N  2 purge  in  order  to  completely remove the  solvent.
texts.text: at  50°C:  and  (c)  heating under  a  N 2  purge  to  600°C for  carbonizing the  polymer.  A  detailed description of the carbonization process  is given elsewhere [1,2,5-7]. Only  a  single coat of  the  latex and  a  single heating operation were  used.  The PTMSP membrane was produced  by:  (a)  coating the  bore  side  of  the alumina support  with a 0.5 wt% solution of the polymer in toluene; (b) air  drying;  and (c)  final drying  under  dry N  2 purge  in  order  to  completely remove the  solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  1 shows a schematic  diagram  of  the  membrane  test apparatus. The coated  alumina  tube  was supported within  a  cylindrical membrane holder (2.3 cm i.d. and  40 cm long). The high  pressure feed  gas to  be separated was passed  through the  bore  side  of the  membrane. The permeated  gas was withdrawn through the  shell side (low  pressure membrane holder side)  in a countercurrent direction relative to feed gas  flow.  A countercurrent pure  CH 4  sweep gas  was used  in  the  shell side  for some of  the  tests. The feed and sweep gas inlet flow rates  were controlled by mass  flow controllers. The  compositions  and the quantities of high and low pressure  side  effluent gases  were measured as functions of times  using, respectively, a gas  chromatograph and a set  of  bubble  flowmeters.
texts.text: Fig.  1 shows a schematic  diagram  of  the  membrane  test apparatus. The coated  alumina  tube  was supported within  a  cylindrical membrane holder (2.3 cm i.d. and  40 cm long). The high  pressure feed  gas to  be separated was passed  through the  bore  side  of the  membrane. The permeated  gas was withdrawn through the  shell side (low  pressure membrane holder side)  in a countercurrent direction relative to feed gas  flow.  A countercurrent pure  CH 4  sweep gas  was used  in  the  shell side  for some of  the  tests. The feed and sweep gas inlet flow rates  were controlled by mass  flow controllers. The  compositions  and the quantities of high and low pressure  side  effluent gases  were measured as functions of times  using, respectively, a gas  chromatograph and a set  of  bubble  flowmeters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: flow rates were varied  in  order  to  obtain  different steady  state performances. All  runs  were conducted at  ambient  temperature (23  _+  1°C). A  feed  gas  mixture  containing 20%  H2, 20%  CH4,  8%  C2H4,  8% C2H6,  29%  C3H 6 and 15%  C3H 8 at  a pressure of 3.0  atm was used  as  the  representative of  the  FCC off-gas. The tests for  this feed  gas  were conducted using  a  pure  CH 4 sweep  gas  at  a  pressure of  1.0  atm. The  ratio  of sweep  to feed gas flow rates  was maintained  at  0.15.  A  feed  gas mixture  containing 35%  H2, 55%  CO 2 and 10%  CH 4 at  a pressure of 4.0  atm was used  as  the  representative of  the  hydrogen PSA  off-gas. The tests for  this feed  gas were conducted  without  using  any sweep gas.  The  low pressure side  of  the  membrane was maintained at  1.0 atm.  Both  the  SSF and  the  PTMSP  membranes were subjected to permeation  tests using  the above described feed  gas  streams. The component  and  overall mass balances closed within  _+  3%.

A  feed  gas  mixture  containing 20%  H2, 20%  CH4,  8%  C2H4,  8% C2H6,  29%  C3H 6 and 15%  C3H 8 at  a pressure of 3.0  atm was used  as  the  representative of  the  FCC off-gas. The tests for  this feed  gas  were conducted using  a  pure  CH 4 sweep  gas  at  a  pressure of  1.0  atm. The  ratio  of sweep  to feed gas flow rates  was maintained  at  0.15.  A  feed  gas mixture  containing 35%  H2, 55%  CO 2 and 10%  CH 4 at  a pressure of 4.0  atm was used  as  the  representative of  the  hydrogen PSA  off-gas. The tests for  this feed  gas were conducted  without  using  any sweep gas.  The  low pressure side  of  the  membrane was maintained at  1.0 atm.  Both  the  SSF and  the  PTMSP  membranes were subjected to permeation  tests using  the above described feed  gas  streams. The component  and  overall mass balances closed within  _+  3%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  addition, the  PTMSP  membrane was fabricated on the  bore  side  of  another sample  of  macroporous alumina  support (Support B) which  was significantly more permeable  to  pure  gases. The same method of preparation as  described earlier was followed except that  a 2.0  wt%  solution of  the  polymer  in  toluene was used.  Both PTMSP membranes (Support A  and B) were examined by a scanning  electron microscope and they  had polymer layer  thicknesses between  9 and 10 /~m.
texts.text: In  addition, the  PTMSP  membrane was fabricated on the  bore  side  of  another sample  of  macroporous alumina  support (Support B) which  was significantly more permeable  to  pure  gases. The same method of preparation as  described earlier was followed except that  a 2.0  wt%  solution of  the  polymer  in  toluene was used.  Both PTMSP membranes (Support A  and B) were examined by a scanning  electron microscope and they  had polymer layer  thicknesses between  9 and 10 /~m.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas permeances (P/U) of N2,  CH 4  and C3H 8 through  the  bare supports  (A  and B) were directly measured by flowing  the gas through  the bore  side at  a  pressure of  pH  while  maintaining the shell side  at  a  pressure of pL. The permeance [ill/ =  j/(pH  _ pL)]  was then  estimated by monitoring the  steady-state flux  of  the  pure  gas  (J)  through  the support. It  was found  that the  bare  support B exhibited ~ 15  times larger  permeance over the bare support A for all three pure  gases. Thus,  the  gas  flow resistance through  support B was more than  an  order of  magnitude  smaller than  that through  support A.
texts.text: Pure gas permeances (P/U) of N2,  CH 4  and C3H 8 through  the  bare supports  (A  and B) were directly measured by flowing  the gas through  the bore  side at  a  pressure of  pH  while  maintaining the shell side  at  a  pressure of pL. The permeance [ill/ =  j/(pH  _ pL)]  was then  estimated by monitoring the  steady-state flux  of  the  pure  gas  (J)  through  the support. It  was found  that the  bare  support B exhibited ~ 15  times larger  permeance over the bare support A for all three pure  gases. Thus,  the  gas  flow resistance through  support B was more than  an  order of  magnitude  smaller than  that through  support A.

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 3.  Separation  of  multicomponent gas  mixtures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance  of  the  SSF  and PTMSP membranes  for  enrichment of  H 2 from  the  FCC  and  PSA off-gases were  evaluated in  terms  of  rejections of  the non-hydrogen  components from the feed  gases  as functions of  H 2  recovery. The rejection of  a  feed  gas component i (R  i) is  defined by the  fraction of  that component (relative to  feed  gas)  leaving the  membrane at  the  low pressure side  (Eq. (3)).  The H 2 recovery (H) is  defined by the  fraction of  feed  gas hydrogen  leaving the  membrane at  the  high  pressure side.
texts.text: The performance  of  the  SSF  and PTMSP membranes  for  enrichment of  H 2 from  the  FCC  and  PSA off-gases were  evaluated in  terms  of  rejections of  the non-hydrogen  components from the feed  gases  as functions of  H 2  recovery. The rejection of  a  feed  gas component i (R  i) is  defined by the  fraction of  that component (relative to  feed  gas)  leaving the  membrane at  the  low pressure side  (Eq. (3)).  The H 2 recovery (H) is  defined by the  fraction of  feed  gas hydrogen  leaving the  membrane at  the  high  pressure side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeances  and the  permselectivities of  the components  of  a  gas  mixture through the  nanoporous carbon  membrane  exhibiting selective surface flow mechanism for  gas  transport and separation depend on many variables. They are: (a) the  partial pressures of  the  components  at  the high  and  low  pressure sides of  the  membrane; (b)  the  corresponding multicomponent  adsorption equilibria on the  membrane  surface; (c)  the multicomponent  surface  diffusivities along the walls of the carbon pores;  (d) the hindered diffusion of  smaller molecules through the  void  space in  the  carbon pores (hindered by  adsorbed molecules); and  (e) the  non-homogeneity of  the  membrane structure.  These properties vary along  the  length  of a tubular membrane  used for  practical separation of gas mixtures. The overall separation characteristics of the membrane  are,  therefore, described by the rejection-recovery of the components (as defined above) of the gas mixture  being separated. It  is possible to  measure  the  permeances  and permselectivities of  the  components  of  a multicomponent gas mixture  under  a specific partial pressure gradient of the  components  across the  membrane using  a  differential test (constant partial pressures at  high  and  low pressure sides). However,  it is  impossible to  measure the  variation in  these  properties over  all  conditions
texts.text: The permeances  and the  permselectivities of  the components  of  a  gas  mixture through the  nanoporous carbon  membrane  exhibiting selective surface flow mechanism for  gas  transport and separation depend on many variables. They are: (a) the  partial pressures of  the  components  at  the high  and  low  pressure sides of  the  membrane; (b)  the  corresponding multicomponent  adsorption equilibria on the  membrane  surface; (c)  the multicomponent  surface  diffusivities along the walls of the carbon pores;  (d) the hindered diffusion of  smaller molecules through the  void  space in  the  carbon pores (hindered by  adsorbed molecules); and  (e) the  non-homogeneity of  the  membrane structure.  These properties vary along  the  length  of a tubular membrane  used for  practical separation of gas mixtures. The overall separation characteristics of the membrane  are,  therefore, described by the rejection-recovery of the components (as defined above) of the gas mixture  being separated. It  is possible to  measure  the  permeances  and permselectivities of  the  components  of  a multicomponent gas mixture  under  a specific partial pressure gradient of the  components  across the  membrane using  a  differential test (constant partial pressures at  high  and  low pressure sides). However,  it is  impossible to  measure the  variation in  these  properties over  all  conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (pressure and composition) existing in a practical membrane system. Thus,  the  design and  performance of  a SSF membrane is  best  evaluated by measuring its  actual  separation performance  (recovery-rejection)  instead  of estimating component permeance and  permselectivity at  any  given  condition. This  is  in contrast with  the  design  of  many conventional polymeric  membranes where component  permeance  and permselectivities are  not  functions of  gas  phase  partial pressures.
texts.text: (pressure and composition) existing in a practical membrane system. Thus,  the  design and  performance of  a SSF membrane is  best  evaluated by measuring its  actual  separation performance  (recovery-rejection)  instead  of estimating component permeance and  permselectivity at  any  given  condition. This  is  in contrast with  the  design  of  many conventional polymeric  membranes where component  permeance  and permselectivities are  not  functions of  gas  phase  partial pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Higher rejections of the non-hydrogen  components  of  the  feed  gas  mixture for  a given  H 2 recovery  are  the  desired performance characteristics of  the membranes for  the  present applications. These  properties can be used to calculate the high and low pressure side  effluent flow  rates and their compositions  by component and overall mass balances  as follows:

These  properties can be used to calculate the high and low pressure side  effluent flow  rates and their compositions  by component and overall mass balances  as follows:

The higher is  the value  of  YP2'  the  larger is  the  hydrogen enrichment by the  membrane.

texts.orig: Eq.  (5) shows that  Pi  for a  non-hydrogen component  of  the  feed  gas mixture  can be decreased by increasing the  rejection of that  component by the membrane. Pi for  hydrogen, on the  other hand,  can be  increased by decreasing the  rejection of  hydrogen by the  membrane.
texts.text: Eq.  (5) shows that  Pi  for a  non-hydrogen component  of  the  feed  gas mixture  can be decreased by increasing the  rejection of that  component by the membrane. Pi for  hydrogen, on the  other hand,  can be  increased by decreasing the  rejection of  hydrogen by the  membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.  M e m b r a n e - P S A hybrid  process  for  production of  pure hydrogen
texts.text: 4.  M e m b r a n e - P S A hybrid  process  for  production of  pure hydrogen

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The high pressure effluent from the membrane can  be  further purified to  produce  an  essentially pure hydrogen  product by using  a  pressure swing  adsorption (PSA) system. Such  hybrid SSF membrane-PSA systems are described in Refs.  [3-7]. It  may  be necessary  to further compress the high pressure membrane effluent gas  to  a  pressure level of  100-300 psig  in order  to  improve the  separation efficiency (lower  adsorbent inventory and higher hydrogen  recovery) of  the  PSA  system  for  H 2 production. The hydrogen  recovery (hydrogen  in  product/hydrogen in feed)  by the PSA  system increases as the H 2 composition  of the feed gas to the PSA system increases [14]. For  example,  the  H 2 recoveries of  a conventional PSA  system  treating a  feed  gas  mixture containing H20, CO2, CO, CH 4 and  H 2  and  producing  99.99  + %  H 2 are  71,  60 and 48%  for feed  gas H 2  concentrations (mole%)  of  60,  50  and  40,  respectively. Thus, the hydrogen recovery  by the PSA system  is  a  strong function of  hydrogen  purity in  the feed  gas.  At the  same time  the  adsorbent inventory of the PSA  process  decreases  as the amounts of non-hydrogen impurities being  treated by  the  process are  decreased. In particular, only  limited quantities of  strongly adsorbed  impurities like propane, propylene  and ethylene are  preferred in  the  PSA  feed  gas because  of  the  difficulty in  desorbing these  impurities from  the  adsorbent [14].
texts.text: The high pressure effluent from the membrane can  be  further purified to  produce  an  essentially pure hydrogen  product by using  a  pressure swing  adsorption (PSA) system. Such  hybrid SSF membrane-PSA systems are described in Refs.  [3-7]. It  may  be necessary  to further compress the high pressure membrane effluent gas  to  a  pressure level of  100-300 psig  in order  to  improve the  separation efficiency (lower  adsorbent inventory and higher hydrogen  recovery) of  the  PSA  system  for  H 2 production. The hydrogen  recovery (hydrogen  in  product/hydrogen in feed)  by the PSA  system increases as the H 2 composition  of the feed gas to the PSA system increases [14]. For  example,  the  H 2 recoveries of  a conventional PSA  system  treating a  feed  gas  mixture containing H20, CO2, CO, CH 4 and  H 2  and  producing  99.99  + %  H 2 are  71,  60 and 48%  for feed  gas H 2  concentrations (mole%)  of  60,  50  and  40,  respectively. Thus, the hydrogen recovery  by the PSA system  is  a  strong function of  hydrogen  purity in  the feed  gas.  At the  same time  the  adsorbent inventory of the PSA  process  decreases  as the amounts of non-hydrogen impurities being  treated by  the  process are  decreased. In particular, only  limited quantities of  strongly adsorbed  impurities like propane, propylene  and ethylene are  preferred in  the  PSA  feed  gas because  of  the  difficulty in  desorbing these  impurities from  the  adsorbent [14].

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: M. Anand et al. /  Journal of Membrane  Science 123  (1997)  17-25
texts.text: M. Anand et al. /  Journal of Membrane  Science 123  (1997)  17-25

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall H 2  recovery of  the  membrane-PSA hybrid  operation for  production of high purity  H 2 from a waste gas is given  by the product  of the individual H 2  recoveries of the  membrane  and the PSA systems.  Consequently,  the preferred performance of  the  membrane system  in  the  hybrid process should  involve: (a)  medium to  high  mole  fractions of H 2 at  high  H 2 recovery; and (b)  low mole fractions and molar flow rates  (high  rejections) of propane, propylene and ethylene in  the  high  pressure effluent gas.
texts.text: The overall H 2  recovery of  the  membrane-PSA hybrid  operation for  production of high purity  H 2 from a waste gas is given  by the product  of the individual H 2  recoveries of the  membrane  and the PSA systems.  Consequently,  the preferred performance of  the  membrane system  in  the  hybrid process should  involve: (a)  medium to  high  mole  fractions of H 2 at  high  H 2 recovery; and (b)  low mole fractions and molar flow rates  (high  rejections) of propane, propylene and ethylene in  the  high  pressure effluent gas.

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 5.  Experimental  results and analysis

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 5.1. Separation of  hydrogen-hydrocarbon mixtures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  2 shows the  hydrocarbon (C1-C  3)  rejections by the  SSF  membrane  (Support  A) as functions of hydrogen  recovery for  the  FCC  off-gas as  feed. The hydrocarbon  rejections are  in  the  order  of  C  3  > C  2 > C ]  for  a given  recovery of  H 2  which  is  consistent with  the  selectivities of  adsorption of  these gases  on the  carbon. It  may  be seen  that very  high  rejections of C 2 and C 3 components of  the  feed  gas can be achieved  at  large  H 2 recoveries. The rejections of alkanes  and alkenes  (propane-propylene or  ethaneethylene) are, however,  comparable  for  a given  carbon  number. The  rejection of CH  4  by  the SSF membrane is  only  moderate.
texts.text: Fig.  2 shows the  hydrocarbon (C1-C  3)  rejections by the  SSF  membrane  (Support  A) as functions of hydrogen  recovery for  the  FCC  off-gas as  feed. The hydrocarbon  rejections are  in  the  order  of  C  3  > C  2 > C ]  for  a given  recovery of  H 2  which  is  consistent with  the  selectivities of  adsorption of  these gases  on the  carbon. It  may  be seen  that very  high  rejections of C 2 and C 3 components of  the  feed  gas can be achieved  at  large  H 2 recoveries. The rejections of alkanes  and alkenes  (propane-propylene or  ethaneethylene) are, however,  comparable  for  a given  carbon  number. The  rejection of CH  4  by  the SSF membrane is  only  moderate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 3  shows  the performance  of the PTMSP membrane  (Support  A) for  H 2 enrichment  from an identical FCC off-gas  feed stream.  The  order  of rejections of  the  hydrocarbons (C  3  > C  2  > C 1)  for  a given  H 2 recovery is  similar to  the  case  of  the  SSF membrane. The absolute numbers for  rejections of C1-C 3  hydrocarbons for  a  given  H 2 recovery by the PTMSP membrane  are,  however,  much  lower  than those  for  the  SSF  membrane using  an identical support material. Table  1  compares  these  absolute rejection  numbers for  a hydrogen  recovery of  60%. The SSF membrane provides a much  higher  rejection of C 2 and C 3  components  than  the  PTMSP membrane. Only the  methane rejections by the  two membranes are  relatively close.
texts.text: Fig. 3  shows  the performance  of the PTMSP membrane  (Support  A) for  H 2 enrichment  from an identical FCC off-gas  feed stream.  The  order  of rejections of  the  hydrocarbons (C  3  > C  2  > C 1)  for  a given  H 2 recovery is  similar to  the  case  of  the  SSF membrane. The absolute numbers for  rejections of C1-C 3  hydrocarbons for  a  given  H 2 recovery by the PTMSP membrane  are,  however,  much  lower  than those  for  the  SSF  membrane using  an identical support material. Table  1  compares  these  absolute rejection  numbers for  a hydrogen  recovery of  60%. The SSF membrane provides a much  higher  rejection of C 2 and C 3  components  than  the  PTMSP membrane. Only the  methane rejections by the  two membranes are  relatively close.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table  1  also  shows the  hydrocarbon rejections by the  PTMSP membrane on Support  B  at  a H 2 recovery of 60%.  The  hydrocarbon  rejections are improved  but  they  are  still substantially lower  than  the corresponding rejection by the  SSF membrane (Support  A). The relatively inferior performance  of  the PTMSP membrane on support A  may be caused  by higher  resistance of  gas  flow  through  that support as mentioned  earlier, which results in  concentration polarization of  the  larger (slow  diffusing) molecules  at the  membrane support interface. It is  obvious  that the use  of  Support  B  for  the  SSF membrane would also improve  its hydrocarbon  rejections over  those  measured  for Support  A  for  any  given  H 2 recovery.
texts.text: Table  1  also  shows the  hydrocarbon rejections by the  PTMSP membrane on Support  B  at  a H 2 recovery of 60%.  The  hydrocarbon  rejections are improved  but  they  are  still substantially lower  than  the corresponding rejection by the  SSF membrane (Support  A). The relatively inferior performance  of  the PTMSP membrane on support A  may be caused  by higher  resistance of  gas  flow  through  that support as mentioned  earlier, which results in  concentration polarization of  the  larger (slow  diffusing) molecules  at the  membrane support interface. It is  obvious  that the use  of  Support  B  for  the  SSF membrane would also improve  its hydrocarbon  rejections over  those  measured  for Support  A  for  any  given  H 2 recovery.

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 5.2. Separation of H2-PSA off-gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  4 shows the  CO 2 and  the  CH 4 rejections as functions of  the  H  2 recoveries by  the  SSF membrane (Support  A) for  the  hydrogen  PSA  off-gas as  feed. The rejection of  CO 2 is  much  larger than  that  of CH  4  because  CO 2 is  more selectively adsorbed  on the  carbon. The relative CH 4  rejection from  this feed gas is  higher  than  that  for  the FCC  off-gas case because  of  the  absence  of  the  higher  hydrocarbons, which suppress CH  4 adsorption on the  carbon.
texts.text: Fig.  4 shows the  CO 2 and  the  CH 4 rejections as functions of  the  H  2 recoveries by  the  SSF membrane (Support  A) for  the  hydrogen  PSA  off-gas as  feed. The rejection of  CO 2 is  much  larger than  that  of CH  4  because  CO 2 is  more selectively adsorbed  on the  carbon. The relative CH 4  rejection from  this feed gas is  higher  than  that  for  the FCC  off-gas case because  of  the  absence  of  the  higher  hydrocarbons, which suppress CH  4 adsorption on the  carbon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 gives the  performance of  the  PTMSP  membrane  (Support A) under  identical conditions of  operation to  those of  Fig. 4.  This  membrane  also  rejects more  CO 2 than CH 4  for a  given H 2  recovery. However, the  absolute values  of  rejections of  these components  are  much  lower  than  those for  the  SSF membrane. Table  2 compares  these  two membranes for  a H 2 recovery of  60%  from the  hydrogen  PSA off-gas. The  SSF  membrane  surpasses the performance of  both  PTMSP membranes (Support A  and B) by far. Both the  rejections of  CO 2 and CH  4 and the  concentrations of  H  2  in  the  high  pressure effluent  gases  are  much  higher  for  the  SSF  membrane compared to those  for  both of the  PTMSP membranes. The PTMSP membrane on Support  B exhibited better CO 2  and  CH  4 rejections than  the  PTMSP

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membrane on Support  A but  the  differences were  not as  pronounced  as  the  FCC  off-gas case.
texts.text: membrane on Support  A but  the  differences were  not as  pronounced  as  the  FCC  off-gas case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The above  results show that both  the  SSF and  the PTMSP membranes exhibit similar separation characteristics for enrichment  of H 2  from FCC and hydrogen PSA  off-gases, but the SSF  membrane provides much larger rejections of  the  non-hydrogen components  of  the  feed  gas  mixture  under  identical conditions of  operation using  identical supports. It also  produces  a  high  pressure effluent with  a  higher concentration of H 2 and lower concentrations of non-hydrogen  components which substantially enhances  the  separation efficiency of  the  companion PSA  unit  in a membrane-PSA  hybrid  system  for production of  high  purity H 2 product.
texts.text: The above  results show that both  the  SSF and  the PTMSP membranes exhibit similar separation characteristics for enrichment  of H 2  from FCC and hydrogen PSA  off-gases, but the SSF  membrane provides much larger rejections of  the  non-hydrogen components  of  the  feed  gas  mixture  under  identical conditions of  operation using  identical supports. It also  produces  a  high  pressure effluent with  a  higher concentration of H 2 and lower concentrations of non-hydrogen  components which substantially enhances  the  separation efficiency of  the  companion PSA  unit  in a membrane-PSA  hybrid  system  for production of  high  purity H 2 product.

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 6. Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The selective surface flow  (SSF) membrane  can be used to  enrich  the  hydrogen  concentration of  a gas stream  containing minor amounts of  H 2 (<  40 mole%)  and bulk impurities like  methane,  ethane, ethylene, propane,  propylene, carbon  dioxide, etc. Very high  rejections of  the  bulk  impurities can be achieved  even at  a low (<  50 psig) feed  gas  pressure.  The hydrogen-rich gas  is  produced  at  the  feed gas pressure. The relatively low concentrations of feed  gas  impurities in  the  high  pressure effluent gas, even  at  large values of  H 2  recovery (> 50%), makes the  use  of  a  SSF membrane-PSA hybrid system  very attractive for  production of  high  purity H 2 from a waste  gas.
texts.text: The selective surface flow  (SSF) membrane  can be used to  enrich  the  hydrogen  concentration of  a gas stream  containing minor amounts of  H 2 (<  40 mole%)  and bulk impurities like  methane,  ethane, ethylene, propane,  propylene, carbon  dioxide, etc. Very high  rejections of  the  bulk  impurities can be achieved  even at  a low (<  50 psig) feed  gas  pressure.  The hydrogen-rich gas  is  produced  at  the  feed gas pressure. The relatively low concentrations of feed  gas  impurities in  the  high  pressure effluent gas, even  at  large values of  H 2  recovery (> 50%), makes the  use  of  a  SSF membrane-PSA hybrid system  very attractive for  production of  high  purity H 2 from a waste  gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SSF membrane can  be produced  in  a  tubular form  using  a macroporous  alumina  tube  as  the  support. This  form of  the  membrane  has  been successfully  tested  for  hydrogen enrichment  applications from Fluid  Catalytic Cracker  and Hydrogen-PSA off-gases at  low  pressures.
texts.text: The SSF membrane can  be produced  in  a  tubular form  using  a macroporous  alumina  tube  as  the  support. This  form of  the  membrane  has  been successfully  tested  for  hydrogen enrichment  applications from Fluid  Catalytic Cracker  and Hydrogen-PSA off-gases at  low  pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  separation characteristics of the polymeric poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membrane  for the  above  described applications are  very  similar to those  of the SSF  membranes. It  also  selectively rejects the  heavier bulk  impurities from a hydrogen containing stream.  The extents of  rejections of  the hydrocarbons and carbon  dioxide for  a  given  hydrogen recovery  by the  PTMSP membrane  are,  however,  much  less than  those  by the  SSF  membranes under  identical conditions of  operations.
texts.text: The  separation characteristics of the polymeric poly-trimethylsilylpropyne (PTMSP)  membrane  for the  above  described applications are  very  similar to those  of the SSF  membranes. It  also  selectively rejects the  heavier bulk  impurities from a hydrogen containing stream.  The extents of  rejections of  the hydrocarbons and carbon  dioxide for  a  given  hydrogen recovery  by the  PTMSP membrane  are,  however,  much  less than  those  by the  SSF  membranes under  identical conditions of  operations.
